attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of red, scaly patches on her neck, upper back and hands. The lesions are painless but occasionally itchy. She has no other symptoms and feels otherwise well. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She works as a gardener and observes that her rashes appear worse after sunny days at work. Her family is not known to have any rashes. She complains of sore joints at times.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are erythematous plaques over her neck, upper back and hands, with areas of hypopigmentation. There is no oral ulceration or joint swelling.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus (SCLE), including photosensitive, non-scarring erythematous plaques in sun-exposed areas. Cutaneous lupus erythematosus may occur as acute, intermittent, subacute or chronic presentations, and may be associated or separate to systemic lupus erytehematosus. It is a chronic autoimmune dermatological condition with unknown cause.

SCLE typically presents with either papulosquamous (psoriasiform) or annular lesions that may result in hypopigmentation but do not cause scarring. The condition is strongly associated with anti-Ro/SSA antibodies, and there may be other types of cutaneous lupus erythematosus lesions concurrently. Arthralgia and arthritis can also be associated with SCLE.

Psoriasis can present with erythematous plaques with silvery scale, but typically affects extensor surfaces like elbows and knees, and is not specifically photosensitive. The scalp and nails are often involved, and there is no post-inflammatory hypopigmentation.

Polymorphic light eruption (PMLE) is a common photosensitivity disorder that presents with pruritic papules, vesicles, or plaques within hours of sun exposure. Unlike SCLE, PMLE lesions typically resolve within days to weeks without residual changes and tend to recur seasonally.

Dermatomyositis can present with photosensitive rashes including the pathognomonic heliotrope rash and Gottron's papules. However, it is typically accompanied by proximal muscle weakness and other systemic symptoms, which are absent in this case.

Tinea corporis presents with scaley lesions but not typically affected by sun exposure or associated with hypopigmentation and arthralgia.

Management of SCLE includes strict photoprotection, topical corticosteroids, and antimalarial medications such as hydroxychloroquine. Regular monitoring is important as patients may develop systemic manifestations of lupus erythematosus though is rarely severe.
learningPoints: Subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus typically presents with photosensitive, non-scarring, papulosquamous or annular lesions in sun-exposed areas.
Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies are present in approximately 70% of patients with subacute cutaneous lupus erythematosus.
references: Grönhagen CM, Nyberg F. Cutaneous lupus erythematosus: An update. Indian Dermatol Online J. 2014 Jan;5(1):7-13. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3937495/
Saracino A. Cutaneous lupus erythematosus. The Australasian College of Dermatologists. 2024. Available at: https://www.dermcoll.edu.au/atoz/cutaneous-lupus-erythematosus/
